preFix.title: Uncial Hand
preFix.subtitle: Insular half-uncial with a broad-edge pen at 10 to 20 degree nib angle
preFix.excerpt: The uncial hand is a round, upright script written with a broad-edge pen held at a shallow angle of 10 to 20 degrees to the horizontal. It is the foundation of many Western calligraphic traditions and remains one of the most legible display scripts.
preFix.sourceNotes: Johnston, Edward. Writing and Illuminating and Lettering. London: Pitman, 1906. Uncial hand, nib angle, and stroke order.

content.text: The
uncial

hand was the dominant book script of the early medieval West. Manuscripts such as the Book of Kells and the Lindisfarne Gospels are written in insular half-uncial, a slightly smaller, more energetic variant. The script is compact, nearly circular in its bowl shapes, and flows naturally from the broad-edge pen at a shallow
nib angle
of 10 to 20 degrees.
Equipment and ink

Use a Mitchell Roundhand No. 3 nib for practice at a body-height of about 5 mm. This nib width allows the stroke contrast to be seen clearly without demanding the precision of a smaller nib. Fit it into an oblique or straight pen holder; either works for uncial because the script is upright and requires no forward lean.

Use black writing ink (sumi or iron-gall). Waterproof drawing inks are too thick for broad-edge calligraphy nibs and will clog quickly. The ink should flow freely when the nib is pulled across the paper without pressure.
Ruling up

Rule guidelines at a body height of 4 nib widths (measure the nib width by making a staircase of horizontal strokes). Uncial is written between a baseline and a waistline with ascenders and descenders extending 2 nib widths above and below respectively. The line spacing should be generous: allow at least 3 body heights between baselines to prevent ascenders and descenders from colliding.
Pen hold and nib angle

Hold the pen so the flat edge of the nib sits at 10 to 20 degrees to the writing line: almost horizontal, with only a slight tilt. Verify the angle by making a horizontal stroke; it should produce a line about 80 to 90 percent of the full nib width. A vertical stroke at the same angle produces a much narrower line. This strong contrast between horizontals and verticals is the defining visual characteristic of the uncial hand.
Never rotate the nib during a stroke. The
ductus
for every letter assumes a fixed nib angle throughout each stroke.
Key letterforms and stroke order
Letter a

The uncial 'a' is one of its most distinctive letterforms: a single-storey shape with a fully closed bowl and a short curved exit at the top right. Ductus: stroke 1 begins at the 1-o'clock position on the bowl, curves left and down in a clockwise arc, rounds the base, and sweeps up the right side to close the bowl at the 3-o'clock position. Lift. Stroke 2 is a short curved exit stroke pulling away to the right from the top of stroke 1. The bowl should be nearly circular.
Letter d

Uncial 'd' has a rounded bowl and a distinctively tilted ascender that leans slightly backward (toward the left). Ductus: stroke 1 makes the full bowl as for 'a'. Lift. Stroke 2 is the ascender: begin at the top-right of the bowl, pull a long stroke upward and slightly left, finishing about 2 nib widths above the waistline. The ascender serif, if used, is a short horizontal stroke at the top.
Letter g

Uncial 'g' has a closed bowl above the baseline and a curved descender below. Ductus: stroke 1 traces the bowl as for 'a'. Lift. Stroke 2 begins at the base of the bowl and curves downward and to the left in a shallow arc, finishing with the stroke pointing approximately to the 7-o'clock position. The descender extends about 1.5 nib widths below the baseline. Do not close the descender into a loop: uncial 'g' has an open-tailed descender.
Exemplar: letter a

attrs.definition: The uncial 'a' is a single-storey letterform. Stroke 1: begin at 1 o'clock on the bowl, sweep counter-clockwise and down in a full rounded arc, close the bowl at 3 o'clock. Stroke 2: exit stroke curving right from the top of the bowl. Nib angle 10 to 20 degrees throughout. Body height 4 nib widths.

content.text: Practice sequence
Warm up with rows of 'o': the simplest expression of the uncial bowl shape. Count the strokes and check that the oval is consistent from letter to letter.
Work through the bowl letters: o, c, e, a, d, g. All derive from the same basic arc.
Work through the arch letters: n, m, h, u. The arch in uncial is rounded, not pointed.
Practice letter spacing: uncial is written with even white space between letters, not even space between the letterforms themselves.

items.fix: Lower the nib angle. Draw a short test horizontal and compare its width to the full nib edge. Adjust the hold until they nearly match.
items.cause: Nib angle too high (tilted toward vertical). At 10 to 20 degrees the horizontal stroke should be nearly as wide as the full nib.

items.fix: Touch the nib to an absorbent surface to remove excess ink before writing. If ink consistently floods, add a drop of gum arabic to the ink to increase viscosity slightly.
items.cause: Ink too thin or nib overfilled.
items.symptom: Ink runs too quickly and floods the stroke

items.fix: Broad-edge nibs should always be pulled in the direction of travel. Strokes that go upward against the cut of the tines will catch. Check that each stroke in the ductus moves the nib in a pulling direction.
items.cause: Nib tines misaligned or the nib is being pushed rather than pulled.
items.symptom: Nib catches and sprays on the down stroke

initialFindings.message: tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
